title: Buckwheat blini
subtitle: Russian yeasted buckwheat pancakes
excerpt: Small, yeasty, slightly sour pancakes made with buckwheat flour, served with soured cream and smoked salmon. The authentic Russian blini — earthier and more complex than the plain flour version.
sourceNotes: Traditional Russian blini are yeasted pancakes made with a significant proportion of buckwheat flour, which gives them their characteristic earthy, slightly bitter flavour and dense texture. The yeasted batter produces a slight sourness that distinguishes them from plain crêpes. Blini are associated with Maslenitsa (Butter Week), the Russian carnival before Lent, when they are eaten in abundance with butter, soured cream, and caviar. The version served at Western events with cream cheese is a simplified commercial adaptation; the yeasted buckwheat original is quite different.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooled in stacks with baking paper between each. Reheat in a dry pan for 1 minute per side or wrapped in foil at 160°C for 10 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make a large batch and freeze for later use.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can be made and left to rise, then refrigerated overnight. Bring to room temperature before cooking.

content.text: Buckwheat blini require patience: the yeasted batter needs an hour to rise, and the first blini from a cold pan is always a test piece that gets eaten in the kitchen. After that, each blini takes about 90 seconds, and a good rhythm means you can produce 20 pancakes in 30 minutes. They should be cooked small (7 to 8 cm across) and thick enough to hold a spoonful of topping without folding.

The buckwheat-to-plain-flour ratio matters: all buckwheat makes a brittle, intense pancake that is hard to flip; half and half is the right balance — earthy and slightly sour from the buckwheat, but pliable enough to handle.

Mix the {{yeast-dried}} and {{granulated-sugar}} into the warm {{whole-milk}} and leave for 5 minutes. Whisk in the {{buckwheat-flour}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, the egg yolks from the {{eggs}}, and the melted {{unsalted-butter}} until smooth. Cover and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until the batter has risen and is bubbly. Just before cooking, whisk the egg whites to soft peaks and fold gently into the batter.

Heat a small knob of butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Drop tablespoons of batter into the pan to form rounds about 7 cm across. Cook for 60 to 90 seconds until bubbles appear across the surface and the edges look set, then flip and cook for 45 seconds more. Stack on a warm plate, covered. Serve with {{soured-cream}}, {{smoked-salmon}}, and {{dill}}.

items.symptom: Blini dense and flat, not light
items.cause: Yeast not active, or egg whites not folded in
items.fix: Check the yeast is active by confirming the batter bubbles after an hour. Fold the egg whites gently — overworking deflates them. The finished batter should look slightly foamy

items.cause: Pan not hot enough or not enough butter
items.fix: The first blini always sticks — discard it. Add a small knob of butter before each batch and ensure the pan is fully hot before pouring the batter

items.symptom: Blini too bitter from the buckwheat
items.cause: Too high a proportion of buckwheat flour
items.fix: Use no more than 50% buckwheat flour in the blend. If the batch tastes too intense, serve with extra soured cream to balance the earthiness

content.text: Blini are Maslenitsa food: the week before Orthodox Lent when pancakes are eaten every day as a symbol of the sun and the coming of spring. Served with butter, soured cream, caviar, and jam, they are made in enormous quantities and consumed over the course of the week. Outside of Maslenitsa, blini appear as a starter at dinner parties (with smoked salmon instead of caviar) and as a light lunch. The yeasted buckwheat version is considerably more interesting than the plain flour pancake that often appears under the same name in the West.
